speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number two-- Mrs. Magda Weiss. Obviously your grandfather, at some point, had to find out that your grandmother was in jail. How did that affect him?

speaker: interviewee
text: By this time, my grandfather was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] very sick. Because, though we don't not told him, he know we are not telling him the truth. And he just found out this himself, or he was told by other Jews. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he was very weak, but he was carrying on and hoping that she come home.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did they keep your father in jail?

speaker: interviewee
text: They keep my father in jail almost until they took us to Auschwitz. He was never allowed to come home. And he was taken to the barracks when all the Jews from there-- all the Jews was taken to Auschwitz. And he was there for five days. And after this, we taken to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: What had happened to your brothers? Where were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: Both of my brothers was in soldiers' camps. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] One was taken out from the country, probably in Ukraine. He's missing.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was Charles. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My other brother was also in soldiers' camp, though he run away. He ran to Budapest. But he was catched and taken back. He lived through the life-- the war. And he emigrated to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the conditions at home with your mother and you when your father was taken to jail?

speaker: interviewee
text: We was allowed to stay home for another-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] three weeks. And after, we have to give up our home. We was got one day to get ready for this, to moving some of our things. And this was the time when they took all the Jews to another place, which was called the Sighet. This is, again, the island. They gathered the Jews there to get at the one who was home. And from there, we was taken to the barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was 1944-- when?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was 1944 in April.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you when they took you out of your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: 17, between 18.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember that day that you left your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I remember this day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: We did find a mover who took us our bed, or something like this, mother and me. But we was ready for everything. And we did have one-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] my brother's wife, the one who was taken to Ukraine, was living in the Sighet all the time. So we went and moved with her family. And there, was we staying maybe five days before we was taken to the barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were your sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sisters was married. One was in Sopron, and the other one was hiding in Budapest.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were staying in Sighet? That was where the ghetto was? Was it a kind of ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: There where the ghetto was, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the ghetto secured? What was it like there?

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't a ghetto. It was really private houses, because most of the Jews lived there. And the one whose home was taken usually find someone who get them into their own houses, or you just stay with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the conditions there?

speaker: interviewee
text: The condition was there very crowded. Because, say, when we was moving into my brother's wife, they did not have a large home. Usually they have maybe-- what you got, a room and a kitchen. Because European life is different. And this all she have, with one child. She have a child.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was your grandfather?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandfather still at his house, the same place, in Sighet, very close to us. He insist to waiting for grandmother. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And there, he was not too far, a few houses farther than where we were.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your mother's state of mind at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's state of mind was very, very good. She want me to hide, too. She was very happy that one of my sisters is hiding. One of my father's workers offered her, and to me, to hiding me. And she really want me to go with this, but I decided I'm not going with her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I wouldn't leave my mother alone anymore. I was ready for this. Plus, I was scared, also, to go with someone I hardly know. But mother know him. He was a student in my father's workshop.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't remember his name. But coincidentally, he was a soldier also in the jail, where my father was staying. And at night, when nobody see it, he come and gave us messages from father. He was really risking his own life to do this.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in the ghetto then?

speaker: interviewee
text: About five days.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what I was like to point out, which is a very hard thing for all the women endure, which was happening. Because before, we was taken to the barracks-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they wanted everything you have in gold-- everything. So they take you to one place, and they said, you still have gold, and you did not give it to us. There was about-- man in one room and a woman in the other. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, and they really decided, where could you hide gold if it's not on your neck, not on your hand, then in your bum?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] First they looked through mother. They didn't find anything in her, because we wouldn't do things like this. And then I have to go through with this. And it was a very torturous things to do. And I never forgot my mother screaming and pain when it was happen with me, because I was roughly, roughly treated. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it's happened with most of the young girls. If they could, they'd destroy you, or half destroy you, so you never be the same woman again. Because even if you were hurt, there was no doctor, there was no help.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this what happened with the women endure.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they was very disappointed if they don't find this gold. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But before we went into this, we have some gold in our necklace, some ring and things. We gave them-- this to them, but this wasn't enough. And they was really known by the Hungarian government and everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were Hungarians who were doing this?

speaker: interviewee
text: They was Hungarian. But they was worse than the German.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know any of these people personally?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- I-- I know one of the person, but I just know whom he was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you badly hurt, physically, by that experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very badly. Very badly. My hurt-- not as much-- I was badly hurt, but to seeing the first time my mother suffered through was bad. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to you then, after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, after this-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was over--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go when you left that building?

speaker: interviewee
text: We couldn't go nowhere. We have to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] go back where we was lived in my brother's wife's house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you talk, you and your mother, on the way back?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I don't remember whether we talked, because we still hardly, hardly gone home, but we wasn't too far from where we lived. This happened in Sighet.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went home, and my poor mother put me in bed and tried to do whatever she can. And we was another five days in this place when they gathering together about three, four transfers of Jews, say maybe 200, 300, one of a time. And then they took you to the barracks. What were the barracks? Where were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: The barracks was in a highway, usually. This was meant for soldiers, used to live there, like wooden building. You was allowed to take some parcels and clothes with you, like blankets and things. And we have to sleep in the floors. We got some food there for about another five, six days before we was taken to the wagons to go to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you bring anything, any personal thing with you from home that you had with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No-- nothing. Because anything we took to Auschwitz, it was taken from us anyway. And when you arrived to Auschwitz, they undress you, are naked, cutting your hair. And either you go to one side to live, either you go to the other side, which we did not know. They are one side for life, the other side maybe have a chance to survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you were taken to the barracks, did you get to see your grandfather again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me about that meeting?

speaker: interviewee
text: By then, my grandfather wasn't able to walk. Because the barracks from the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] from the Sighet was a long way. So he had to have some transportation. By then, he got-- his eyes got very bad. His walking got very bad. He was very, very sick. So we couldn't comfort him, because we don't have comfort ourselves. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- he was taken by transfer to the barrack.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was-- all the men who was in the jail was taken to the barracks right from the jail. It was probably, I would think, maybe 50 or something like close. There we met our father again, like, we were together, mother, father, and us, but not with our grandfather.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your grandmother as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: And grandmother, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the trip to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: We was taken by closed wagons. I wouldn't exactly know how many in one wagon-- maybe 80 to 100. But I don't know exactly the numbers. And it was very, very closed and hot in these wagons. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My mother felt sick. She was having this kind of heat waves. I wasn't too bad myself. Neither was my father as bad as mother. Mother was sick. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] if I remember right, it was taken six to seven days till we reach Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you see when you got there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was kind of barrack like of some building, and sign-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what kind of a building where they are. There was a Jewish person come and help us to throw out our parcels.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they gave you kind of an advice, what you saying. And you don't say, if you were younger, how young you are. You just have to say you have to be 17 at least, because otherwise, I think, you'll be killed. But they talked to you personally, but slowly, but who would understood the meaning of this?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then there was the German soldiers, who was-- tell you, you go to the right, you go to the left. Mother goes here. And the men was, again, has to go on their own direction. And that's how we were separated. We could say a few words to each other. My mother asked me not to say no to the Germans, because she don't want I'll be beaten again, like some of the other person was who wasn't separated. My mother asked me, do not do this. Because she just don't feel like this should happen again. My father say goodbye to me. He also said, probably, you might stay in life. You have a chance. Hold on, whatever happen. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But I think the other one-- all of us would be killed. He knew it in a moment he stepped foot in Auschwitz. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] after this, we was taken again to a barrack-- at least I was. I don't see Mom or Dad anymore. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We was about 1,000 in the barrack. I was taken after they cut my hair and take all my clothing and just gave me one rag to cover myself. This was all-- nothing else. After we went in a barrack, there was Polish girls, who was there for five years. They told us we are very lucky, because they have been in here in five days, and they told us everything they could.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they tell you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they told you, you are in a death camp. You might have a chance to go to work. But this is not for sure. And the kind of food and the kind of life we have to live there. One sleep-- we sleep on the floor again. One has their head down one way. The other, you know, had their foot. And every morning, there was an appell. When the German come, they counting us. We have to stay five in line. And there were quite few mother who was let stay in life, but their children had been taken from them. And they was very, very sick after their children's-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] About two or three weeks later, I was taken back to another place from this barrack, to a second barrack. And maybe with 50 numbers on this barrack, we was again looked over by the Germans. What happened with the rest, I do not know, but about 15 or so was taken back to the barracks where I was taken.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was Mengele, who was again selected the girls. And I was taken back. And again, everybody questioned, what happened? What happened with the others? But we had no answer.

speaker: interviewee
text: The only thing-- they just don't believe they send us back, and they did not know for why. It's happened one more times-- same thing. And the third times happened again. This time, I was sent to Reichenbach for a working place.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long all together were you in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Six weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was when in '44? What month?

speaker: interviewee
text: '44, May. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] maybe the fifth days and about five to six weeks-- more six weeks than five weeks, when I was taken from Auschwitz this time. If they won't take me this time, I wouldn't last much longer in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I was sick, and when I went to, you know, to the washroom, blood start to come from my-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] stomach. And this was pretty well-- if it's starting, you'll be taken to the crematorium anyway. Because how long could you hold on?

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you think was wrong with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was my nerve-- loneliness, sadness, the food and the cold-- where you sleep at night, which was cold, and daytime, the sun was killing you. It was really hot at daytime. And I don't really know what more.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do about this illness?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Nothing. Whenever you have any illness, you just kept quiet, because if you tell them or if they know it themselves, you look sick, that's the end of your life. And I wanted to live through, to hoping just to live through. Because my father also told us, when he saw me the last, it couldn't last too long, maybe another four weeks. And you might be able to hold on. He was wrong. It was lasted another year.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of a place was Reichenbach? [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Reichenbach is a smaller city, I think, was somewhere near Poland, or was maybe in Poland, more like Poland than Germany. It was a city where all the prisoners working, many, many thousands of them in different camps they was living. The most famous was the [INAUDIBLE]. And it was about three, four kilometers from the place I used to work. But where I live, it was almost about a 3/4 hour's walk only from the factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of a factory was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: A radio factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of work did you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was making lamps for radios or for boats. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And made a different part of these complicated lamps. Were all the prisoners working for these same factories? Or were there different kinds of factories there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. There was different kind of factories, many of them. I only knew the one I was working, because you're coming, talking with the other prisoners. Some was working fieldwork. And-- and in different factories, but they was all manufacturing for the war something, except the field workers.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of conditions were in the places where you stayed, where you were living?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where I was living it was the best condition, I was told, whatever they gave to the prisoners, because this was a special lager where they even brought in the Red Cross sometimes, to show how the prisoners lived. But this place was only maybe 100 Jewish prisoners and maybe 60 Dutch prisoners. All the others didn't have the way we was living, because we have our bunk bed with straw and a blanket. And we also able to wash ourselves daily.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I stop you there for a moment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.
